gps_placement.title: Introduction — 3 Sentences

items.text: In recent years, ...
While some believe ..., others feel that ...
In this essay, I will examine both views and explain why ...
It is generally accepted that ...
Although some support ..., others feel that ...
Personally, I strongly agree that ...

gps_placement.feedback: Hook = general opening about the topic area. Background = narrows to the debate or problem (often shows two sides). Thesis = states YOUR plan or position for the essay.

gps_placement.title: PEEEL Body Paragraph — All 5 Components

items.text: The main reason for this is ...
This is simply because ...
In Australia, for instance, ...
Without this, ... would inevitably ...
Clearly, this demonstrates that ...

gps_placement.feedback: P = the claim you're making. E (Explain) = the mechanism (WHY/HOW). E (Example) = specific evidence with names, places, data. E (Effect) = what happens as a result (so what?). L = confirms your thesis.

gps_placement.title: The Tricky Pair: Point vs. Explanation

items.text: One major factor is ...
This occurs because ...
Perhaps the most significant concern is ...
Given that ..., ...
The other contributing factor is ...
This means that ...

gps_placement.feedback: Ask yourself: Am I NAMING an issue, or explaining HOW it works? Point = 'The problem is X.' Explanation = 'This happens because Y.' If you just replaced the Point in different words, it's NOT an Explanation — it must add NEW information about the mechanism.

gps_placement.title: The Other Tricky Pair: Example vs. Effect

items.text: Indeed, in the USA, distracted driving is the number one cause of...
If these issues are left untreated, it is inevitable that ...
Research conducted by ... found that ...
Such initiatives have had considerable success in ...
Take ... as an example.
This would inevitably lead to ...

gps_placement.feedback: Example = SPECIFIC evidence — it contains names, places, numbers, data. It PROVES what you already said. Effect = what HAPPENS next — the consequence, the chain reaction, or the 'if not' scenario. Example looks backward (proof). Effect looks forward (what next?).

gps_placement.title: UPEPE Body Paragraph — All 5 Components

items.text: There are two main causes of weight problems among children.
The first one is poor diet.
Indeed, youngsters today consume vast quantities of fat, sugar and salt in fast food, which inevitably leads to weight gain.
The other major factor is sedentary lifestyles.
Modern children tend to spend their day sitting in front of screens, meaning they cannot burn the calories they consume.

gps_placement.feedback: Umbrella says 'there are two reasons' (general). P1 names the FIRST. E1 does double duty — it explains WHY and gives evidence in one go. P2 transitions to the second. E2 does the same double duty for Point 2. ⚠️ Each Extend slot packs both explanation AND example/evidence together.

gps_placement.title: UPEPE: Umbrella vs. Point 1

zones.label: P1 — Point 1 (specific — names the FIRST idea)

items.text: There are several key benefits stemming from ...
The main one is the greater access it allows.
Criminals commit offences after punishment for several reasons.
One major factor is their background.
Challenging though this problem is, there is much that can be done.
The first step in curbing the damage is ...

gps_placement.feedback: If the sentence says 'there are two/several [reasons/benefits]' without naming them → Umbrella. If the sentence says 'the first is [specific thing]' → Point 1. The Umbrella opens the roof; Point 1 puts the first item under it.

gps_placement.title: Between-Paragraph Transitions

items.text: There are others, however, myself included, who feel that ...
Challenging though this problem is, there is much that can be done to ...
There are, however, some drawbacks associated with ...
Despite these arguments, however, ...
To combat this, governments need to ...
Despite these benefits, there are also serious negative outcomes ...

gps_placement.feedback: Each essay type has its own Body Paragraph 2 transition. Discussion = shift to the other side's view. Problem-Solution = shift from problems to solutions. Adv-Disadv = shift from positives to negatives (or vice versa).

gps_placement.title: Conclusion — 3 Components

items.text: In conclusion, ...
Although ... may seem ..., ...
Greater investment from governments is urgently needed.
To conclude, ...
While ... has been a long tradition, ...
Efforts should instead be made to ...

gps_placement.feedback: Signal = 'In conclusion' (always start here). Concession = acknowledge the other side briefly ('Although X...'). Recommendation = your final call to action or strongest restatement. The concession shows balance; the recommendation shows authority.

b1_b2_upgrade.b2: The main reason is... / The other factor is...
b1_b2_upgrade.tip: Examiners see 'Firstly, Secondly, Thirdly' as a memorised template. Vary your Point starters.
'Also' is too informal for academic writing. 'In addition to this' shows sophisticated additive cohesion.
'But' at sentence start is too casual. Use 'However' (new sentence) or 'Although' (same sentence).
'So' is conversational. 'As a result' signals academic cause-effect reasoning.
Rotating between 'for instance,' 'Indeed,' and 'Take X as an example' shows Lexical Resource variety.

b1_b2_upgrade.b2: This is because ... / This occurs because ...
b1_b2_upgrade.tip: Starting with 'Because' often creates fragments. 'This is because' is complete and academic.

b1_b2_upgrade.b2: In my view, ... / Personally, I feel that ...
b1_b2_upgrade.tip: 'I think' is Band 5. 'In my view' is Band 7+. Small change, big impact.

b1_b2_upgrade.b1: There are many reasons for this.
b1_b2_upgrade.tip: Generic umbrellas score low. Always include the TOPIC in your umbrella sentence.
'The other contributing factor' avoids mechanical numbering and sounds natural.
'Clearly' is the examiners' favourite Link opener. It closes a paragraph with authority.
'In conclusion' is the standard in every examiner essay. Don't try to be creative here.

b1_b2_upgrade.b1: Some people think X. Other people think Y.
b1_b2_upgrade.b2: While some believe ..., others feel that ...
b1_b2_upgrade.tip: Combining both views into ONE complex sentence shows grammatical range — a key Band 7+ skill.

paragraph_assembly.title: PEEEL — Opinion Essay (Zoos)

sentences.text: Although many zoos have made great improvements, there can be no doubt that captive animals suffer.
sentences.label: Point

sentences.text: Not only are the enclosures usually far too small, but the climate and conditions are often completely unsuitable.
sentences.label: Explanation

sentences.text: Polar bears, for instance, travel across vast distances daily and yet, in some tropical regions, they are kept in small pens.
sentences.label: Example

sentences.text: In addition to this discomfort, zoo animals can suffer stress as a result of unfamiliar cage-mates, not to mention noisy visitors.
sentences.label: Effect

sentences.text: Clearly, the welfare of these animals is severely compromised.
sentences.label: Link

paragraph_assembly.failTip: Remember: Point (claim) → Explanation (why/how) → Example (specific proof) → Effect (consequence) → Link (back to thesis).

paragraph_assembly.title: PEEEL — Problem-Solution (Crime)

sentences.text: Despite the seriousness of the reoffending problem, there are a number of possible ways to remedy the situation.
sentences.label: Point

sentences.text: The key factor is the treatment of the underlying issues.
sentences.label: Explanation

sentences.text: In many cases, this may mean addiction rehabilitation in prison or in the community.
sentences.label: Example

sentences.text: Providing education programs or training can enhance employability, which would dramatically reduce their risk of reoffending.
sentences.label: Effect

sentences.text: Clearly, addressing root causes is more effective than punishment alone.
sentences.label: Link

paragraph_assembly.failTip: The Explanation tells us WHAT the key factor is. The Example gives a specific case. The Effect shows the RESULT of that action.

paragraph_assembly.title: UPEPE — Causes (Childhood Obesity)

sentences.text: There are two main causes of weight problems among children.
sentences.label: Umbrella

sentences.text: The first one is poor diet.
sentences.label: Point 1

sentences.text: Indeed, youngsters today consume vast quantities of fat, sugar and salt in fast food, which inevitably leads to weight gain.
sentences.label: Extend 1 (explain + evidence)

sentences.text: The other major factor is sedentary lifestyles.
sentences.label: Point 2

sentences.text: Modern children tend to spend their day sitting in front of screens, meaning they cannot burn the calories they consume.
sentences.label: Extend 2 (explain + effect)

paragraph_assembly.failTip: UPEPE order: Umbrella (covers both) → Point 1 → Extend 1 (explain + prove it) → Point 2 → Extend 2 (explain + prove it). Each Extend does DOUBLE DUTY — it explains WHY and gives evidence.

paragraph_assembly.title: UPEPE — Solutions (School Behaviour)

sentences.text: Clearly, in order to manage the problem, it must be tackled both at school and at home.
sentences.label: Umbrella

sentences.text: On an institutional level, school authorities need to get tough on bad behaviour.
sentences.label: Point 1

sentences.text: That means they need to set rules regarding uniforms, homework, and classroom conduct, with consequences like detention if broken.
sentences.label: Extend 1 (explain + example)

sentences.text: On the home front, parents too need to step up.
sentences.label: Point 2

sentences.text: Without expectations and limitations around behaviour, they are setting their child up for a life of trouble.
sentences.label: Extend 2 (explain + effect)

paragraph_assembly.failTip: Notice how 'On an institutional level' and 'On the home front' signal the TWO solutions. Each Extend gives concrete detail about HOW that solution works.

paragraph_assembly.title: Introduction — Discussion Essay (Art Funding)

sentences.text: It is generally accepted that art enriches a society and plays a valuable role in its culture and history.
sentences.label: Hook

sentences.text: Although some support government investment in galleries and artistic organisations, others feel that this money should be spent on public services.
sentences.label: Background

sentences.text: In this essay, I will consider both views and explain why I agree that state funding of the arts should be redirected.
sentences.label: Thesis

paragraph_assembly.failTip: Introduction order is always: Hook (general topic) → Background (the debate/narrowing) → Thesis (your plan/position). The Hook is the widest; the Thesis is the most specific.

paragraph_assembly.title: Conclusion — Opinion Essay (Space)

sentences.text: In conclusion, humanity's thirst for knowledge is unquenchable.
sentences.label: Signal + Restatement

sentences.text: It is time, however, for private corporations to invest.
sentences.label: Concession + Shift

sentences.text: Governments must take responsibility for their real duty here on earth.
sentences.label: Final Position

paragraph_assembly.failTip: Conclusion order: Signal ('In conclusion') → Concession (acknowledge other side) → Final Position (your strongest statement). Always end on YOUR position, not the concession.

essay_type_sort.phrase: In this essay, I will [verb] ... and [verb] ...
essay_type_sort.tip: The TEMPLATE 'In this essay, I will...' is universal — but what comes AFTER changes by essay type. Opinion: '...explain why I agree.' Discussion: '...consider both views.' P-S: '...outline causes and suggest solutions.' A-D: '...outline advantages and drawbacks.'

essay_type_sort.phrase: In this essay, I will consider both views and explain why I agree that...
essay_type_sort.tip: 'Both views' = Discussion essay ONLY. This phrase does two jobs: previews the two-sided structure AND hints at your opinion. In an Opinion essay, you don't present 'both views' — you argue YOUR side. In P-S, you discuss problems and solutions, not views.

essay_type_sort.phrase: Those who support [X] usually argue that...
essay_type_sort.tip: Reporter language is ONLY for Discussion essays. You are reporting OTHER people's arguments neutrally. In Opinion essays, you state YOUR view directly. In P-S and A-D, you describe problems/advantages objectively — no 'arguing' involved.

The Umbrella phrase works everywhere — any time you need to introduce two ideas in one paragraph. Just change the noun: 'reasons' (Opinion/Discussion), 'causes' (P-S), 'benefits/drawbacks' (A-D), 'factors' (Two-Part).

essay_type_sort.phrase: Others, however, myself included, feel that...
essay_type_sort.tip: The 'myself included' switch is unique to Discussion essays — it marks the shift from Reporter mode (BP1) to Judge mode (BP2). No other essay type uses this Reporter→Judge transition.

essay_type_sort.phrase: Challenging though this problem is, there is much that can be done.
essay_type_sort.tip: This concessive solution opener is specific to Problem-Solution structures. It acknowledges the problem ('Challenging though...') then pivots to solutions ('...can be done'). Two-Part works if one part asks for solutions. You would NOT use this in Opinion, Discussion, or A-D.

'Indeed' is the most universal evidence introducer. It means 'in fact / truly' (NOT 'for example'). It works in every essay type, every paragraph position where you present evidence. [F:10+] — the most frequent examiner phrase in the entire corpus.
'Clearly' is universal — it closes ANY paragraph by confirming what the paragraph proved. It's the examiner's favourite Link word. [F:8+] across all essay types.

essay_type_sort.phrase: There are, however, some drawbacks associated with...
essay_type_sort.tip: This transition specifically shifts from advantages to disadvantages. The word 'drawbacks' signals an A-D structure. Not used in Opinion (you argue one side), Discussion (you present views, not advantages), or P-S (you present problems, not drawbacks). Two-Part ONLY if the question specifically asks for advantages and disadvantages.

essay_type_sort.phrase: Despite these benefits, there are also serious negative outcomes...
essay_type_sort.tip: Another advantages→disadvantages transition. The pattern 'Despite these [positives], [negatives]' is the signature move of an A-D essay. It would sound odd in any other essay type because it frames the discussion as 'benefits vs. negatives.'

Opinion essays need YOUR position throughout. Discussion essays need your opinion in BP2/BP3 or the conclusion. Problem-Solution and pure Adv-Disadv stay OBJECTIVE — no personal opinion needed. ⚠️ Exception: A-D 'Outweigh' questions DO need 'In my view' because they ask for evaluation. Two-Part: only if one question asks for your opinion.

essay_type_sort.phrase: While some believe ..., others feel that ...
essay_type_sort.tip: This 'two sides' Background pattern is for Opinion and Discussion essays — both present a debate between views. Problem-Solution and A-D essays use a DIFFERENT Background: 'This has led to growing concerns about...' or 'One of the results of this has been...' They introduce a problem/situation, NOT two opposing views.

essay_type_sort.phrase: Efforts should instead be made to...
essay_type_sort.tip: Recommendations suit Opinion (asserting what should change) and Problem-Solution (proposing action — this is the natural conclusion for P-S). Discussion essays end with a balanced judgement, not a strong recommendation. A-D conclusions weigh the two sides. Two-Part: only if one question asks for solutions.
The most universal additive connector. Works as glue in any paragraph, any essay type. It replaces the B1 habit 'Also, ...' with proper academic cohesion. [F:7+]

essay_type_sort.phrase: In this essay, I will outline [the causes] and suggest [solutions].
essay_type_sort.tip: This thesis pattern previews BOTH halves of a Problem-Solution essay: causes/problems in BP1, solutions in BP2. It is NOT used in Opinion or Discussion (which don't discuss 'causes and solutions'). Two-Part works if the question has a causes+solutions structure.

essay_type_sort.phrase: This essay will discuss the main advantages and potential drawbacks of...
essay_type_sort.tip: This thesis previews the two halves of an Advantages-Disadvantages essay. Notice: no 'I believe' — pure A-D essays stay objective. The phrase 'advantages and drawbacks' signals A-D structure specifically. It would NOT fit in any other essay type.

pairs.demo: A junk food diet inevitably leads to weight gain.
The majority of crime in Australia stems from drug and alcohol addiction.
This can give rise to greater prejudice.
Parents supporting adult children, resulting in increased costs.
Our changing lifestyles have had a far-reaching effect on our health.
This has a detrimental effect not only on individuals but on society.
This could have a significant positive impact on health.
This can have long-term benefits for their self-esteem.
Investment is crucial to bridge this gap.
There has been a dramatic increase in the amount earned.
The anxiety can be severe.
This effort is ultimately futile.
Education programs can enhance employability.
Poor parenting exacerbates problems at school.
Steps need to be taken to eliminate these energy sources.
Suggest ways to curb bad behaviour.
Many schools are facing a variety of challenges.
There can be no doubt that captive animals suffer.
This debris poses potential risks to future missions.
Children who play can reap benefits which serve them well.

upgrade_ladder.demo: A junk food diet inevitably leads to weight gain.
upgrade_ladder.tip: "Leads to" = #1 causation verb. Direction: X → Y.

upgrade_ladder.demo: The majority of crime stems from drug and alcohol addiction.
upgrade_ladder.tip: "Stems from" traces back to the ROOT cause. Direction: Y ← X.

upgrade_ladder.ctx: This has had a _____ on education.
upgrade_ladder.demo: Our changing lifestyles have had a far-reaching effect on our health.
upgrade_ladder.tip: "Far-reaching" = affects many areas. For WIDE impact.

upgrade_ladder.ctx: Social media has a _____ on mental health.
upgrade_ladder.demo: This has a detrimental effect not only on individuals but on society.

upgrade_ladder.ctx: Investment is _____ to bridge this gap.
upgrade_ladder.demo: Investment is crucial to bridge this gap.
upgrade_ladder.tip: One word swap: "important → crucial." Instant Lexical Resource upgrade.

upgrade_ladder.demo: The anxiety this may create can be severe.
This would dramatically reduce their risk of reoffending.
This would inevitably lead to a reduction in revenue.
upgrade_ladder.tip: "Inevitably" = cannot be avoided. Perfect for Effect sentences.

upgrade_ladder.ctx: The _____ of teenagers use social media.
upgrade_ladder.demo: The vast majority of food outlets are fast food restaurants.
upgrade_ladder.tip: Hedges your claim so examiners see nuance, not over-generalisation.

upgrade_ladder.demo: Individuals may feel compelled to keep up with others.
Many parents foster a disregard for authority, which exacerbates problems.

upgrade_ladder.ctx: Steps must be taken to _____ these energy sources.
upgrade_ladder.demo: The first step must be to phase out extraction and use.

upgrade_ladder.ctx: Crime is a _____ in most countries.
upgrade_ladder.demo: Crime is a pervasive problem in most countries around the world.

vocab_gps_mapping.tip: You are NAMING an issue. Noun phrases go in Point sentences.

vocab_gps_mapping.ph: leads to / stems from / gives rise to
vocab_gps_mapping.tip: Causation verbs show the MECHANISM — HOW things work. Explanation's job.
Evidence with names, data, research = Example. You're PROVING your claim.
Impact nouns show the CONSEQUENCE. "What happens as a result?"
Evaluative adjectives JUDGE significance. "Clearly, this is crucial."
Change verbs show HOW a process intensifies. Explanation territory.
Intensity adverbs amplify consequences. "This would INEVITABLY lead to..."

vocab_gps_mapping.ph: the vast majority / a significant number
vocab_gps_mapping.tip: Quantifiers present SCALE in evidence. Example territory.
You are NAMING what matters. Point sentence territory.

vocab_gps_mapping.ph: this would inevitably lead to / if left unaddressed
X faces challenges / X poses risks
vocab_gps_mapping.tip: Existence verbs NAME a situation — "Schools face challenges" is a Point that names the problem.

vocab_gps_mapping.ph: There are two key drivers of...
vocab_gps_mapping.tip: UPEPE Umbrella sentences need QUANTIFIER nouns (two, several) + CATEGORY nouns (drivers, causes, benefits). They FRAME both ideas.
UPEPE Extend slots pack explain + evidence in ONE sentence. They need BOTH verb collocations AND noun phrases working together.

shared_vs_specialist.ph: leads to / stems from / contributes to
shared_vs_specialist.tip: Causation verbs work in ALL 5 essay types. Highest-ROI items in the bank.
Impact nouns are universal — every essay type needs Effect sentences.
Evaluative adjectives work everywhere: Points, Links, Conclusions.
Intensity adverbs amplify any claim in any essay type.

shared_vs_specialist.ph: a pervasive problem / a growing concern
shared_vs_specialist.tip: Problem nouns are universal. Every essay type can name problems.
Change verbs work everywhere. They show HOW things get better or worse.

shared_vs_specialist.ph: the vast majority / a growing number / individuals
shared_vs_specialist.tip: Quantifiers and academic people-words are universal. Every essay type needs hedging and formal register.

shared_vs_specialist.ph: X faces challenges / X poses risks / X suffers from
shared_vs_specialist.tip: Existence verbs are universal. "Poses risks" appears in ALL 5 types in the Shareability Matrix.

shared_vs_specialist.ph: it is claimed / supporters argue / it is felt
shared_vs_specialist.tip: Reporter verbs are ONLY for Discussion essays — reporting others' views neutrally. In Opinion, you state YOUR view. In P-S, you describe objectively.
Reporter people-nouns name WHO holds each view. Only Discussion essays present named groups of supporters vs. opponents.
The Shareability Matrix shows these in Opinion ✅, P-S ✅, and Two-Part ✅. NOT in Discussion or A-D. They prescribe actions — Opinion essays assert change, P-S essays propose solutions.
Funding verbs are P-S specialists (+Two-Part). They name specific financial actions. You wouldn't "subsidise" in a Discussion or Opinion essay.
The matrix shows "far outweigh" in Opinion ✅ and A-D ✅. It compares two sides on a scale — used in Opinion conclusions and A-D "outweigh" questions.

shared_vs_specialist.ph: a further plus / the downside / a serious drawback
shared_vs_specialist.tip: These nouns FRAME the discussion as advantages vs. disadvantages. "The downside" only makes sense when you've discussed the upside.
Strong assertive language expressing YOUR position. Discussion uses reporter verbs; P-S stays more objective with "should" not "ought to be."

shared_vs_specialist.ph: legitimate concerns / the diversion of funds
shared_vs_specialist.tip: The matrix shows these in Opinion ✅, Discussion ✅, and A-D ✅. They evaluate negatives — useful whenever you judge a downside, regardless of essay structure.

slots.label: P — Point
slots.tpl: Perhaps the most _____ regarding children's social media use is the impact on their mental health.

slots.label: E — Explanation
slots.tpl: This _____ the constant comparison with peers' curated lives online, which triggers anxiety.

slots.label: E — Example
slots.tpl: Indeed, research found that _____ teenagers on social media reported symptoms of depression.

slots.label: E — Effect
slots.tpl: If left unaddressed, this _____ young people's self-esteem and academic performance.

slots.label: L — Link
slots.tpl: Clearly, protecting children from these harmful effects is _____.

gps_builder.title: UPEPE — Causes (Problem-Solution)
gps_builder.topic: "What are the causes of childhood obesity?"

slots.label: U — Umbrella
slots.tpl: There are two _____ of weight problems among children.

slots.label: P1 — Point 1
slots.tpl: The first one is _____.

slots.label: E1 — Extend 1
slots.tpl: Youngsters consume vast quantities of fat and sugar, which inevitably _____ weight gain.

slots.label: P2 — Point 2
slots.tpl: The other major factor is _____.

slots.label: E2 — Extend 2
slots.tpl: Modern children spend their day in front of screens, _____ them being unable to burn calories.

gps_builder.title: PEEEL — Discussion Essay (Reporter Mode)
gps_builder.topic: "Some feel zoos should be closed. Discuss both views."

slots.label: P — Point (Reporter)
slots.tpl: _____ that they serve an indispensable role in conservation.

slots.label: E — Explanation
slots.tpl: Breeding rare species in captivity _____ the survival of endangered populations.

slots.label: E — Example
slots.tpl: Indeed, _____ have successfully reintroduced pandas and condors into the wild.

slots.label: E — Effect
slots.tpl: Without these programs, many species would face extinction rather than _____ from conservation.

slots.label: L — Link
slots.tpl: Clearly, the role of zoos in species preservation is _____.

slots.label: U — Umbrella
slots.tpl: There are several _____ that can be taken to address this issue.

slots.label: P1 — Point 1
slots.tpl: Firstly, governments could _____ apprenticeship programmes in key industries.

slots.label: E1 — Extend 1
slots.tpl: This would _____ young graduates' practical skills and make them more attractive to employers.

slots.label: P2 — Point 2
slots.tpl: In addition, authorities could _____ companies that discriminate based on age.

slots.label: E2 — Extend 2
slots.tpl: Such legislation would _____ the disadvantages young people face when competing with experienced candidates.

slots.label: E — Effect (adverb)
slots.tpl: Without practical training, graduates would _____ struggle to compete.

slots.label: E — Effect (noun)
slots.tpl: This could have _____ on both their careers and the economy.

slots.label: L — Link (adjective)
slots.tpl: Clearly, curriculum reform is _____.
